vol_name: Dynamic Sitting and Reaching in Infants
vol_description: The dataset examines the development of infants’ dynamic sitting postural control in the forward and right directions. Infants between the ages of 6 and 10 months participated in this cross-sectional study. All infants were able to sit on the floor for at least 5 seconds. Caregivers encouraged their infants to reach for toys placed forward and to the right. All sessions were conducted via Zoom™ and video-recorded for further analyses using DeepLabCut for 2D pose estimation and Datavyu to determine the timings of the reach task and to code infants’ motor behaviors. Analyses focused on infants’ movements in the anterior-posterior plane (for forward reaches) and the medio-lateral plane (for rightward reaches).

vol_name: Measuring Intervention Effects using User-Defined Video-Coding Assessment:  Examples from a Case Series
vol_description: The purpose of this study was to describe the process of using video-coding in clinical settings and examine its reliability and responsiveness following physical or occupational intervention. We used video examples of 5 children with cerebral palsy (3 to 13 years old) while they performed a functional task pre- and post- intervention. We document the items coded for each child and describe how we applied Datavyu (a commonly used video-coding tool in behavioral research) to video-code the items and how they can be transformed to analyzable and objective observations. Two coders coded the items from the pre- and post- intervention videos to quantify reliability and responsiveness. All coded items were representative of what the clinician and families aimed to measure. Examples include the amount of time both feet were supported on the floor while sitting, the number of physical cues required to don a shirt, etc. Coders were highly reliable, showing discrepancies of ≤136 ms for time-based items and 0 discrepancies for frequency-based items. Moreover, most video-coded items were sensitive to improvements after intervention. Video-coding allows clinicians to record behaviors that are overlooked in standardized assessments and provides opportunities towards more user-defined assessments while maintaining the richness and complexity of functional tasks.

vol_name: Task-specific training to improve sitting in a child with severe postural impairments: A single-subject design
vol_description: Purpose: To evaluate the potential of a task-specific training model to improve sitting through tailored trunk support and incrementally challenging reaching. Methods: A single-subject AB design with follow-up was conducted with a 20-month-old child with severe postural impairments due to 16p11.2 deletion syndrome and multiple comorbidities. Phase A included 6 baseline assessments during 2 weeks, followed by 15 intervention sessions (Phase B) during 5 weeks, and 4 post-intervention assessments during follow-up. Outcomes included the Segmental Assessment of Trunk Control (SATCo) and the Gross Motor Function Measure (GMFM) sitting dimension. Visual analysis, the 2 standard deviation band method, and C-statistics evaluated change. Results: SATCo and GMFM scores were stable during baseline but had significant trend changes from baseline to intervention and follow-up phases. Independent sitting skills emerged after the intervention. Conclusions: Task-specific training tailored to the child’s ability by systematically progressing trunk support and reaching practice may promote long-lasting sitting function.

This study is the first to assess the segmental progression of control of the body for independent standing in typically developing infants and in infants with motor delays. We supply different levels of lower body supports and assess control of the free segments of the body. We hypothesize that control of body segments develops progressively, from control of the segments above the hips to above the ankles, for the acquisition of independent standing. Thus, during the months prior to independent standing, infants will have decreased postural control of the free segments as the support is reduced from hips to ankles. Moreover, the segmental control of the body will be related to their ability to independently stand and their functional status. Results of this study will open new frontiers in the creation of innovative assessments and training programs. If our predictions are correct, an objective frame of work to segmentally train the body with optimal levels of external supports might be used to examine or challenge postural control in children with moderate motor deficits.
